content.full_text:
M- 84-071

State of California

Memorandum

To : Barbara Millman
Chief Counsel
Fair Political Practices
Commission

Date : March 9, 1984

Subject:

From : Governor's Office

This memorandum confirms our conversation of March 9,
1984, regarding the use by the Governor's Chief of Staff of a
State vehicle to attend a meeting of the Republican Party
Central Committee. As I explained, the Chief of Staff is
permanently assigned a State vehicle for his fulltime use.
The vehicle is equipped with radio devices which allow him to
be in constant contact with the Highway Patrol and with the
Office of Emergency Services Command Center. Through the
Command Center he can reach State offices as necessary to
impart and receive information on state business. He commonly
drives the vehicle to all official and unofficial functions
attended by him, unless alternative transportation with suit-
able communications facilities is provided.

The Chief of Staff has been invited and will speak before
an educational forum outside of Sacramento. Before his return
to Sacramento, he plans to speak to the Lincoln Club, a group
affiliated with the Republican Party and will meet with the
Republican Party Central Committee. We discussed whether the
use of the State car assigned to him to travel to the above
events would give rise to a reporting obligation under the
Political Reform Act, or would otherwise be prohibited by the
Act.

It was your opinion that if the car and its associated
communications equipment provided a necessary communications
link between the Chief of Staff and State agencies, it could
be driven to all events which he attends without creating a
reporting obligation or offending provisions of the Act.

Thank you for your prompt advice on this matter. If
the contents of this memorandum departs from your previous
advice, please inform me of the differences as soon as
possible.

Vance W. Raye
Legal Affairs Secretary

VWR:er

State of California

Memorandum

To : Barbara Millman
    Chief Counsel
    Fair Political Practices
    Commission

From : Governor's Office

Date : March 9, 1984

Subject:

This memorandum confirms our conversation of March 9, 1984, regarding the use by the Governor's Chief of Staff of a State vehicle to attend a meeting of the Republican Party Central Committee. As I explained, the Chief of Staff is permanently assigned a State vehicle for his fulltime use. The vehicle is equipped with radio devices which allow him to be in constant contact with the Highway Patrol and with the Office of Emergency Services Command Center. Through the Command Center he can reach State offices as necessary to impart and receive information on state business. He commonly drives the vehicle to all official and unofficial functions attended by him, unless alternative transportation with suitable communications facilities is provided.

The Chief of Staff has been invited and will speak before an educational forum outside of Sacramento. Before his return to Sacramento, he plans to speak to the Lincoln Club, a group affiliated with the Republican Party and will meet with the Republican Party Central Committee. We discussed whether the use of the State car assigned to him to travel to the above events would give rise to a reporting obligation under the Political Reform Act, or would otherwise be prohibited by the Act.

It was your opinion that if the car and its associated communications equipment provided a necessary communications link between the Chief of Staff and State agencies, it could be driven to all events which he attends without creating a reporting obligation or offending provisions of the Act.

Thank you for your prompt advice on this matter. If the contents of this memorandum departs from your previous advice, please inform me of the differences as soon as possible.

Vance W. Raye
Legal Affairs Secretary

sections.question_synthetic: Whether the Governor's Chief of Staff's use of a State vehicle equipped with communications equipment to attend Republican Party meetings and events would trigger reporting obligations or violate the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: According to the memorandum, FPPC Chief Counsel Barbara Millman advised that if the State vehicle and its communications equipment provided a necessary communications link between the Chief of Staff and State agencies, the vehicle could be driven to all events without creating a reporting obligation or violating the Political Reform Act.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request/confirmation memorandum from the Governor's Office rather than an FPPC formal advice letter. The document describes a verbal opinion already given by FPPC counsel. No formal question and conclusion sections exist; information extracted from narrative text. Document appears to be seeking confirmation of prior informal advice.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether the Governor's Chief of Staff's use of a State vehicle equipped with communications equipment to attend Republican Party meetings and events would trigger reporting obligations or violate the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: According to the memorandum, FPPC Chief Counsel Barbara Millman advised that if the State vehicle and its communications equipment provided a necessary communications link between the Chief of Staff and State agencies, the vehicle could be driven to all events without creating a reporting obligation or violating the Political Reform Act.

embedding.summary: This is a memorandum from the Governor's Office to FPPC Chief Counsel confirming a March 9, 1984 conversation regarding whether the Governor's Chief of Staff's use of a State vehicle to attend Republican Party events would require reporting or violate the Political Reform Act.
